coredata.dc:title: Density of states in randomly shaped graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:issueName: Special issue: Proceedings of the 10th International Conference on the Physics of Light–Matter Coupling in Nanostructures, PLMCN 2010 (Cuernavaca, Mexico), 12–16 April, 2010
coredata.dc:creator: Espinosa-Ortega, T.
Luk’yanchuk, Igor A.
Rubo, Yuri G.
coredata.dc:description: By numerical diagonalization of honeycomb-lattice tight-binding Hamiltonian we calculate the density of state (DOS) of irregularly shaped graphene quantum dots fabricated in the form of graphene nano-flakes. The finite-size electron confinement and the edge states result in the central peak of DOS that is located at the zero-energy Dirac point. The amplitude and width of the peak are provided by the form of the graphene cluster, but no regular correlation with its shape was found.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Disorder
Quantum dot
Edge states
Density of states

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Density of edge states in graphene flakes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustrating the model used to construct a randomly shaped graphene flake. The graphene quantum dot is composed of carbon atoms that are inside a random closed curve (thin line) generated using Eq. (1).

Showing the density of states of several graphene flakes. The flakes’ configurations are shown in the inserts together with the total number of carbon atoms N . We also indicate the number of edge states
N
e
, as defined in the text.

The average density of states of several graphene flakes (thick line) and the density of states of ideal infinite graphene (thin line). Both curves are normalized to the same average number of carbon atoms < N > = 988 .

head.ce:title: Density of states in randomly shaped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de Investigación en Energía, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Temixco, Morelos, 62580, Mexico

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Picardie Jules Verne, Laboratory of Condensed Matter Physics, Amiens, 80039, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: By numerical diagonalization of honeycomb-lattice tight-binding Hamiltonian we calculate the density of state (DOS) of irregularly shaped graphene quantum dots fabricated in the form of graphene nano-flakes. The finite-size electron confinement and the edge states result in the central peak of DOS that is located at the zero-energy Dirac point. The amplitude and width of the peak are provided by the form of the graphene cluster, but no regular correlation with its shape was found.

ce:section.ce:para: Discovery of carbon monolayer graphene [1–3] amplified the interest in the charge carriers, having the property of the “massless Dirac fermions”, responsible for the transport properties of graphene and partially of the bulk graphite [4]. The distinguishable feature of these carriers is that, they are described by the linear band spectrum composed from two (electron and hole) conical branches touching each other in the Dirac junction point. This makes them formally similar to the massless relativistic particles in high-energy physics described by the four component Dirac-like equations. Therefore the conducting properties of these carriers should be different from those described by the Schrödinger equation.

In ideal graphene, because of the Dirac-like shape of the spectrum the electronic density of state (DOS) vanishes at Fermi level and forms the linear zero-energy cusp. The impurity doping or application of the gate voltage shifts the Fermi level from its zero point, which provides the conductivity properties of graphene. At the same time the impurity-induced lattice disorder results in the series of localized states located in vicinity of the Dirac point and manifested as the zero-energy delta-like peak in DOS. Although no disorder-induced metal-insulated transition is expected in graphite, these localized states can play the important role in optical and transport properties of graphene.

In current work we shall be interested in confined electronic states in small graphene quantum dots (GQD) fabricated in form of the nano-flakes since in such small nano-clusters the finite-size and surface-induced localized states are dominated in the central zero-energy peak in DOS. The edge states have been previously discussed for graphene nanoribbons [5] and for regularly shaped quantum dots [6,7]. It was shown that the edge states appear on the edges with zig-zag termination whereas they are absent in the armchair edges. The edge states were also studied in regularly shaped graphene quantum rings for different geometries [8].

We shall study the more realistic case of GQD with random termination, where, in principle, both the armchair and zig-zag edge geometries coexist. The objective of the current paper is to obtain the DOS for such nano-clusters and to compare the results for different samples. We shall use the exact numerical diagonalization of the honeycomb-lattice tight-binding Hamiltonian for randomly shaped GQD. The advantage of this technique is that the Dirac-like peculiarity and confined states are obtained simultaneously during diagonalization and no assumption about the initial nonperturbative state of Dirac fermions in the pure infinite systems should be done.

The results are important to understand the optical properties of the GQD. It is known that ideal graphene has the high transmissivity up to 98% from infrared to ultraviolet and high optical conductivity [9]. Fabrication of realistic GQD, susceptible to be used in the solar-energy sources, challenges the question of their electronic structure and, in particularly, the difference in transparency with respect to ideal graphene.

In order to obtain the tight-binding Hamiltonian for the randomly shaped graphene dot, we first develop an algorithm to construct it. The random graphene quantum dot is built using a closed random curve defined by the following dependence of the radius r on the azimuthal angle ϕ ,
(1)
r
( ϕ )
=
R
0
+
∑
n = 1
n
max
[
A
n
sin
( n ϕ )
+
B
n
cos
( n ϕ )
]
,
where
R
0
is the average radius of the curve,
A
n
and
B
n
are random reals generated with a Gaussian distribution and they are substantially smaller than
R
0
[10], and
n
max
∼
R
0
/ d is chosen to avoid fluctuations with the length smaller than the distance d = 1.44
Å
between the nearest-neighbor carbon atoms. We used
R
0
= 10 d and
n
max
= 25 in our calculations presented here.

After the curve is generated we consider the graphene flake to be composed with the atoms that are encircled with the curve (see Fig. 1 ). This way we obtain random graphene flakes containing about 103 carbon atoms.

We construct the Hamiltonian tight-binding matrix in the usual way, and we work with an N × N Hamiltonians in real space, where N ∼
10
3
is the total number of atoms in a given graphene flake. Clearly, this number fluctuates from flake to flake. According to the tight-binding model, the elements in the Hamiltonian are zero except for the site energies
H
i , i
= ε and the nearest-neighbor hopping terms
H
i , j
= t . We do not consider the next nearest-neighbor hopping here. We set ε = 0 and use t = − 3.033
eV
[11].

The atoms on the edges of graphene flakes can have two or only one nearest neighbor, instead of three nearest neighbors for any internal atom. Experimentally, the dangling bonds are typically passivated with hydrogen atoms. In what follows, we assume that the on-site energies of carbon atoms on edges are the same as for atoms in the bulk. In order to set the boundary condition, we demand that the wave function vanishes outside the flake, hence we set the energy of hydrogen sites to be infinite. In this way the probability of transition from the edge carbon atom to the hydrogen atom goes to zero.

Diagonalizing the Hamiltonian we get the energies and the density of states for different graphene flakes. A sharp peak centered at zero energy is observed. This peak is related to the edges states [6,7]. The properties of the edge-state peak are shown in the next section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Density of edge states in graphene flakes
ce:section.ce:para:
In the Fig. 2 we show the density of states, normalized by the number of carbon atoms, for one regular (hexagonal with zig-zag edges) graphene quantum dot and five random graphene flakes. DOS was calculated assuming a Gaussian broadening of each individual energy level with the dispersion σ = 0.03
eV. In order to quantitatively characterize the number of edge states we calculate the area of the peak in the energy interval
[ − Δ , + Δ ]
. The nearest integer
N
e
to this area for Δ = 0.25
eV
is also indicated in the figures. This number, characterizing the intensity of the central peak, fluctuates strongly. In particular, in Fig. 2(c), (e) we have shown two flakes with a relatively small number of edge states, and Fig. 2(b), (d), (f) show the flakes with quite big amount of edge states.

We studied the dependence of
N
e
as a function of different parameters that characterize the edges, such as the total number of edge atoms, the number of edge atoms with only one bulk neighbor, the number edge atoms with two bulk neighbors (like for the atoms on zig-zag edges), and the number of edge atoms with one edge-atom neighbor and one bulk atom neighbor (like for the atoms on armchair edges). We did not find any significant correlations.

In the Fig. 3 we show the density of states averaged over 21 random flakes and compare it with the density of states of infinite ideal graphene. The last was calculated in the same tight-binding model using the known analytical expression via the elliptic integral [12]. This Figure demonstrates the redistribution of the states due to the finite-size effects. One can see that the appearance of the central peak due to the edge states is accompanied by the decrease in the number of bulk high-energy states.

In conclusion, the randomly shaped graphene flakes can exhibit big number of edge-bound states that lead to strong central peak in the density of states. The presence of edge states should be important for the transport and optical properties of disordered graphene films.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by DGAPA-UNAM under the project IN112310. IAL thanks the ANR agency, the project LOMACOQU, and the project FP7 IRSES-TERACAN.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: We remove the curves that are generated with intersections, i.e., we consider only simply connected graphene quantum dots.
